text: The surgeon begins the total laparoscopic hysterectomy by cutting the round ligament using an ultrasonic scalpel, with the large uterus visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon carefully identifies the artery located just below the round ligament and proceeds to treat and safely cut the fallopian tube using a harmonic scalpel.
The surgeon uses bipolar energy to achieve hemostasis on the uterine-ovarian pedicle, preparing to safely cut the ligaments.
The surgeon identifies the addexal vein located just above the ovary, preparing to address this anatomical structure during the hysterectomy procedure.
The surgeon coagulates the adnexal vein using electrocautery, facilitating the opening of the broad ligament. The ovary is preserved due to the patient's young age.
The surgeon uses a uterine manipulator to push the uterus, facilitating the dissection of the bladder. The dissection is performed using gas, resulting in a dry and bloodless separation of tissues.
The assistant retracts the bladder cranially, while the surgeon cuts both round ligaments to increase the mobility of the uterus, preparing for the dissection of the bladder.
The surgeon pushes the uterus to the left side of the patient, performing hemostasis on small bleeders. Connective tissue is cut to enhance uterine mobility, ensuring control over small vessels.

The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to cut the right round ligament, ensuring control of the artery located just below the ligament. Small hemostasis is performed, and the fallopian tube is controlled during the procedure.

The surgeon uses an ultrasonic scalpel to control the fallopian tube and round ligament. The utero-ovarian ligament is controlled with bipolar cautery due to the larger vessels present, ensuring efficient hemostasis.

The surgeon uses bipolar coagulation to safely control the utero-ovarian pedicle while pushing the uterus medially. The corpus luteum is visible on the right ovary. The surgeon takes care to avoid burning the bowel located just below the surgical field.
The surgeon switches to the harmonic scalpel and targets the area previously coagulated with bipolar energy to control the utero-ovarian pedicle.
The surgeon pushes the uterus medially to gain safe access to the utero-ovarian ligament. The harmonic scalpel is used to coagulate the ligament before cutting it.

The surgeon identifies the left adnexal vein and notes the presence of a large vein that requires control with bipolar coagulation before cutting with the harmonic scalpel. The coagulation controls the right adnexal vein, facilitating the opening of the brow pigments for safe access to the uterosacral ligament.
The surgeon cuts the left adnexal vein after controlling it, then moves medially to increase the mobility of the uterus.

The surgeon opens the posterior part of the broad ligament, carefully cutting the peritoneum to expose the uterosacral ligament. The uterosacral ligament is identified, and the peritoneum is being removed to provide clear access.

The surgeon skeletonizes the uterine vessels, identifying the uterine artery and the uterine vein medially. Dissection is performed between the uterine artery and the peritoneum to enhance uterine mobility. The bipolar instrument is used for dissection in the upper part of the left uterosacral ligament.

The surgeon performs a vesico-uterine dissection, carefully opening the Halman plane using a harmonic scalpel. The uterine vessel is visible near the medial part of the bladder pillar, requiring caution to avoid damage during the dissection.

The surgeon identifies the vagico-vaginal plane and coagulates visible vessels to ensure a clear dissection field. Using the harmonic scalpel, the surgeon demonstrates the mobile and fixed parts, opting for the cold part to safely dissect without burning the tissue.

The surgeon controls a small vessel using sharp coagulation before proceeding with bladder dissection. The space between the bladder and the vagina is increased, revealing the medial part of the bladder pillar.

The surgeon performs vascular control on small varicosities in the medial part of the bladder pilar. The anatomy of the bladder pilar is highlighted, showing the upper cranial vascular part and the dorsal neurovascular part.

The surgeon carefully cuts the upper part of the bladder pilar, avoiding ureteral and nerve injury. The uterine artery and vein are visible, requiring caution to prevent damage to these structures during dissection.
The uterine manipulator is positioned, creating space for vaginal closure. The surgeon prepares for uterine vessel control, with visible manipulation of the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon removes small connective tissue surrounding the uterine pedicle to enhance the effectiveness of coagulation, allowing for complete control of the uterine pedicle.

The surgeon applies energy to the vessel using forceps until no bubbles are visible between the arms, indicating the tissue is dry and safe to cut without hemorrhage. The vessel is coagulated over two to three centimeters to minimize bleeding from the remnant uterus.

The surgeon controls the vein using the same technique, ensuring no bubbles are present, indicating effective coagulation. Surrounding tissue is also controlled, and another artery is identified. The cervical vaginal vessel on the left side is controlled over two to three centimeters, before proceeding to the opposite side.

The surgeon is on the right side of the uterus, cutting through the connective tissue to expose the right uterine belly. The technique involves removing surrounding connective tissue to locate the uterine artery.

The surgeon carefully navigates the right part of the uterus, aiming to avoid damage to the large uterine pedicle. A small incision is made to improve access to the artery, followed by the removal of surrounding peritoneum. Short bursts of coagulation are applied to achieve hemostasis, with visible bubbles indicating active coagulation between the forceps arms.

The surgeon efficiently controls the right uterine artery, moving up and down the isthmus. The uterus is lateralized to the opposite side of the pedicle to increase the distance between the uterus and the pedicle. The uterine artery is then cut.

The surgeon uses bipolar forceps to cut the uterine vein positioned between the forceps' arms, taking time to ensure precision. The cervical vaginal pedicle is controlled before performing colpotomy with a hook, using bipolar forceps for hemostasis.
The surgeon carefully retracts the bladder away from the dissection area to minimize the risk of thermal injury. Following this precaution, the surgeon prepares to perform the colpotomy.
The surgeon uses a uterine manipulator and a hook to perform the colpotomy, moving the hook from right to left across the uterine manipulator.
The surgeon cuts the posterior part of the vagina laterally, positioning the incision just above the uterosacral ligaments.

The surgeon extracts a large uterus with a ten-centimeter myoma through the vaginal opening. To close the vagina, a Vicryl Zero suture with a fourteen-millimeter needle is used, starting on the lateral part of the vaginal tissue.

The surgeon carefully incorporates the mucosa into the knot to prevent post-operative bleeding, using an extracorporeal knotting technique with a self-locking rudder knot. The peritoneum is visibly included in the stitch.

The surgeon performs the same procedure on the opposite side, taking the lateral edge of the vagina and identifying a bleeder. The bleeder is secured with a stitch, incorporating the peritoneum into the interrupted suture that encompasses the entire vagina.

The surgeon observes that the bleeding has stopped due to the pressure of the stitch. They proceed to close the medial part of the vagina, carefully positioning the needle to avoid including any part of the bladder in the stitch.
The surgeon performs a suture, inserting the needle into the peritoneum during the closure of the vaginal cuff.

The surgeon performs intracorporeal knotting, creating two flat knots in the same position and one knot on the opposite side. The final knot is placed, securing the anterior and posterior parts of the vagina, with the stitch passing through the peritoneum.
The surgeon closes the vaginal cuff using wire knotting tape, securing the final knot to ensure the vagina is well closed.
The surgeon observes the right ovary, noting its excessive mobility, and performs an ovariopexy to stabilize the ovary and reduce the risk of ovariitis.
The surgeon prepares to perform an ovariopexy on the right ovary, which appears excessively mobile, to reduce the risk of ovarian or adnexal torsion.
The surgeon performs a similar procedure on the opposite side, taking the round ligament, the fallopian tube, and the ovary. The knot is locked to complete the interruption.

The surgeon demonstrates the position of the ureter, which is distant from the working area, ensuring no risk of torsion for the ovary. The ureter is shown with its rotation, and there are no visible bleeders in the surgical field.
